title: Introduction
description:
WELCOME to Living-with-Fire.org

A portal to information and resource links to help you adapt to life with wildfire

I am a retired federal employee who spent 35 years managing fires and natural and cultural resources in national parks and on public lands. I know that we can't stop the tidal wave of wildfires that are upon us, so I am dedicating my time to trying to help others adapt to life with wildfire.

The subject matter is diverse and there is a wealth of resources available on the Internet which can be time consuming to locate. I created this website to facilitate exploration and learning that ideally leads to collaborative action. I make no claims to completeness, authorship, ownership, or proprietary interest in the materials provided unless specifically indicated. Most of the information is already available through an Internet search.

The original website was created to support a course I instructed in 2020 with Mary Kwart, a retired federal fire management specialist. I want to thank Mary for her contributions to the original site and for allowing me to retain some of her materials.

I want to thank Walter Sydoriak for designing, developing, and maintaining the website. I could not have created this tool without him. I also want to thank Annie Lundgren and the White Rabbit Creator Clubhouse for the monumental work done to create this website and rewrite the Plant List Generator for the web.

Charisse Sydoriak, January 9, 2022, February 12, 2026

Why aren’t existing approaches working? Why are we struggling to engage everyone?     Erica Peng emphasizes that a critical gap in wildfire mitigation isn’t a lack of technical solutions but a human-behavior and psychology barrier: our automatic fight-flight-freeze responses can undermine proactive fire safety actions and collaborative mitigation work. She draws on neuroscience and behavioral psychology to explain why ongoing wildfire threat often triggers stress reactions that make people avoid, deny, or resist preparing for wildfire, even when they know the risks. In this YouTube recording, Peng highlights lessons from organizing fuel reduction and evacuation planning in her own neighborhood, and she points to the importance of understanding and regulating stress reactions to motivate stronger engagement in mitigation and communication among community members and leaders. The study found that strong offshore wind regimes produced fast-moving wildfires characterized by rapid spread, long-distance spotting, and intense firebrand showers, making evacuations difficult and increasing loss of life and property. Although the strongest wind and dry conditions often occurred near Southern California population centers, the most destructive and deadly wildfires in the dataset were in less-populated areas of northern California and western Oregon, where communities were more interspersed with flammable fuels, experienced wind-prone electrical ignitions, and included higher proportions of socially vulnerable residents less able to evade rapidly evolving wildfires."}], "title": "Research Articles on How Fire Spreads in the Built Environment"}, {"links": [{"url": "https://firebrandcollective.org/", "title": "1. What it offers:  •\\t“Ready NOW!” neighborhood preparedness model •\\tSupport for forming local wildfire action groups •\\tCommunity wildfire risk assessment assistance •\\tEvacuation planning coordination •\\tFirewise facilitation and neighborhood activation •\\tEmphasis on relationship-based, locally led action Best for: Communities seeking a bottom-up, relational organizing model that builds local ownership and sustained engagement."}, {"url": "https://www.fireadaptedwashington.org/toolkit/", "title": "2. Fire Adapted Washington ", "description": "What it is: A statewide decentralized network that operates through a mix of nonprofit, agency and local community engagement venues to help communities organize wildfire resilience efforts. The model works best for: neighborhood leaders, fire districts, and local champions who want plug-and-play materials for hosting community meetings and launching action groups.   The network offers: • Ready-to-use meeting agendas and slide decks • Facilitator notes and training guides • Topic-specific toolkits (home hardening, evacuation planning, fuels reduction) • Templates communities can customize • Structured pathways for organizing local wildfire groups.  "}, {"url": "https://cafiresafecouncil.org/", "title": "3.  Fire Safe Council (California)", "description": "What it is: In California, a Fire Safe Council is a locally based, community-driven organization—often volunteer-led or nonprofit—that brings together residents, fire agencies, and other partners to reduce wildfire risk through education, hazardous fuels reduction, defensible space projects, and grant-funded mitigation efforts. Supported by the California Fire Safe Council and working in coordination with CAL FIRE, these councils identify local wildfire hazards and implement practical risk-reduction strategies, but they do not have regulatory authority or enforcement power. Instead, they function as collaborative platforms that mobilize communities to take collective action to improve wildfire preparedness and safety. Fire Safe Council governance, membership, and operations varies by county/city. Here is one example: Santa Barbara County Fire Safe Council https://sbfiresafecouncil.org/ "}], "title": "Community Wildfire Risk Reduction--Grassroots/Coalition Models"}, {"links": [{"url": "https://wildfirepartners.org", "title": "1. Boulder County Wildfire Partners ", "description": "What it is: Wildfire Partners is funded through county taxes and is operated by Boulder County. The program is staffed with formalized processes and provides structured assessments, cost-share, and certification. Residents participate, but governance and authority sit with the county.  "}, {"url": "https://marinwildfire.org", "title": "2. Marin Wildfire Prevention Authority (CA)", "description": "What it is: The Marin Wildfire Prevention Authority (MWPA) is a countywide wildfire prevention agency formed in 2020 through a joint powers agreement among Marin County and 17 local jurisdictions to coordinate proactive risk reduction. It is funded by Measure C, a voter-approved parcel tax that provides dedicated revenue for vegetation management, home hardening and defensible space programs, evacuation planning, wildfire detection, and public education. MWPA engages residents through grants, chipper programs, outreach campaigns, and partnerships with nonprofit groups, helping communities take personal mitigation action. While it supports consistent implementation of wildfire safety standards across jurisdictions, enforcement of codes and ordinances remains with the individual member agencies.
